concept.title: Working with multiple rule sets
concept.shortdesc: A single repository can contain content that must meet different editorial standards — for example, a tutorial section and an API reference section with distinct metadata requirements. Use separate rule set files and specify the correct one at runtime with the --rules option.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Learn when and how to use more than one rule set in a single repository: per-product standards, CI matrix jobs, and how to prevent local vs CI rule mismatch.

section.title: When to use more than one rule set
section.p: A single rule set works well when all content in the repository follows the same standards. Use multiple rule sets when:

ul.li: Different content types have different metadata requirements. For example, tutorial articles must have ms.topic: tutorial and a "Prerequisites" section, while API reference articles do not.
Different teams own different subdirectories and maintain their own editorial standards independently.
You are migrating from one standard to another and need to run the old rule set against existing content and the new rule set against new content simultaneously.
One rule set is shared across repositories (for example, a base style standard) while each repository adds its own product-specific rules on top.

section.title: Specifying the rule set at runtime
section.p: The --rules option accepts any path to a JSON rule set file. You can run the validator multiple times against different directories using different rule sets:
section.codeblock:
# Validate tutorials with the tutorial rule set md-validate validate docs/tutorials/ --rules rules/tutorials.json
# Validate API reference with the reference rule set md-validate validate docs/reference/ --rules rules/api-reference.json
section.p: There is no configuration file that automatically selects the rule set. The rule set is always specified explicitly on the command line — either by the author running the tool locally or by the CI pipeline.

section.title: How authors know which rule set applies
section.p: Document the rule set path in your repository's contributing guide. Common conventions:

ul.li: By directory — docs/tutorials/ uses rules/tutorials.json, etc.
By front-matter field — ms.topic value maps to a rule set.
Script wrapper — a validate.sh script picks the rule set automatically based on file path.

section.p: Whatever convention you use, the local path must match the one CI uses.

section.title: CI configuration for multiple rule sets
section.p: When you have two or more rule sets, each one should run as a separate CI job so that failures in one area do not suppress failures in another. Use a GitHub Actions matrix strategy to avoid duplicating the job definition:
section.codeblock:
jobs: validate: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: include: - docs-dir: docs/tutorials/ rules-file: rules/tutorials.json artifact-name: reports-tutorials - docs-dir: docs/reference/ rules-file: rules/api-reference.json artifact-name: reports-reference
steps: - name: Check out repository uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Set up Python uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: '3.12'
- name: Cache NLTK data uses: actions/cache@v4 with: path: ~/nltk_data key: nltk-punkt-tagger
- name: Install markdown-validator run: pip install markdown-validator
- name: Download NLTK language data run: python -m nltk.downloader punkt_tab averaged_perceptron_tagger_eng
- name: Validate documentation run: | md-validate validate ${{ matrix.docs-dir }} \ --rules ${{ matrix.rules-file }} \ --format json \ --output reports/
- name: Upload validation reports if: always() uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4 with: name: ${{ matrix.artifact-name }} path: reports/
section.p: Each matrix entry produces a separate check on the pull request. A failure in tutorials does not prevent the reference check from running and reporting its own result.

section.title: Preventing local vs. CI rule mismatch
section.p: The most common multi-ruleset problem: an author validates with the wrong rule set locally and sees different failures in CI.

ul.li: Commit all rule set files. Never use local copies that diverge from source control.
Document the exact command in the contributing guide, including the full --rules path.
Verify the CI --rules value in .github/workflows/validate-docs.yml matches what authors use locally.
Pin shared rule sets if they are shared via a package or submodule — uncoordinated updates cause hard-to-trace CI failures.

section.p: If local and CI results differ, check that --rules is identical in both. See also Resolve a validation failure in CI.
